Alright, you're back and we are into the thick of it with looping. So what are we? We have two loops down and how many loops to go? Good question, right? I mean, how many loops should you actually run? Three loops? Four loops? Five loops? Ten loops? Twenty loops? Well, before I fully answer that, let me stop by saying that for those prospects who haven't bought yet, you're definitely going to run at least one more loop. Why? Well, you have one more number in that combination that you have not addressed yet, namely, their pain threshold, right? Those, that safe crackers metaphor, right? You spin the dial one way, you hear that click, that's your first ten. What do you do? You spin it the opposite way. You hear a second click, boom, you spin it the third way. Five numbers, right? Well, you only have four so far. We focused on the three tens and we lowered action threshold. Well, there's one more number left, which is that pain threshold. In essence, people who are feeling significant pain tend to act quickly to get out of pain. Conversely, people who are in denial of their pain tend to act slowly. In consequence, it's basically an inverse relationship between the amount of pain a prospect is feeling and their action threshold. Confusing, I can demystify it right now with this perfect metaphor and a slide. Watch this. This is an old-fashioned scale. Check it out. You have the fulcrum right here, this little triangle, right? And you have this called the lever or the beam here, right? Where you put weights on either side, right? So now watch what happens. At the beginning of a sales encounter, the prospect walks in with reasons to buy now the positives sort of speak based on his preconceived notions or her preconceived notions. So there's positives on one side and there's the negatives, which is not to buy now on the other side. And those could be divided up obviously with logical reasons to buy now, emotional reasons to buy now, logical reasons not to buy now, and emotional reasons not to buy now. Positives, negatives, right? And in the beginning, based on their preconceived notions, typically you're going to have some level of excess on the negative side. They're not certain yet. Make sense? So what happens is this, just look at this conceptually. As you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close, every word you say, every action you take, every document and sales aid you use is all designed to do what? You're essentially building up positives, building up positives while you're simultaneously knocking out negatives, knocking out negatives. So the scale starts to tip, right? But remember, these scales have lots of things that you decide is a nurture. They don't tip so easily. So you start moving them down the straight line and eventually when they buy, that's the equivalent of the scale tips and bam, you knocked out enough negatives and are enough positives to make it differential and they said yes. That's what's happening based on the scale, right? But here's the thing. Notice where the fulcrum is. It's right in the middle, dead center. Well when you lower someone's action threshold, what are you actually doing? Well, believe it or not, you're actually moving the fulcrum. See my father, mad math, with his ultra high action threshold, well what does that mean for his fulcrum? His fulcrum is not in the center. His fulcrum is all the way over to the right on the yes side, the good side, right? So what happens if the fulcrum is over here on this side and you add a weight on, it doesn't have much force. Remember with a large enough lever, you can lift the world, right? Well guess what? If it's the fulcrum is all the way over there, every single positive gives a penny weight of force while one little negative corrupts the whole system. So people with high action thresholds like my dad, their fulcrum is all the way over to the right. People like me on the other hand, well my fulcrum is all the way over to the left. In my case, negatives don't have that much power over me and one positive is enough to outweigh them all. Now from the opposite perspective, from running movies, right, well the difference is because of the way I run parallel movies, meaning I run a really long positive one and a very short negative one, that's what causes the fulcrum to be so far over to one side. It's on the negative side. So even one positive tips to scale. Conversely, like that strategy of running this long toxic terrible movie where if he spends 50 bucks on the big broke destitute and hated by the world and want to kill himself basically, right, want to just like commit Harry Carrie, well he's all the way on the positive side, the fulcrum. So all those positives you add on doesn't have much effect because that one nasty negative tips to scale the other way. So what happens when you run that action threshold lowering pattern by hijacking their strategy of running that really long toxic negative movie and then allowing them for the first time to experience the wonder and benefits of running a long positive movie with each words you say, the fulcrum starts sliding back towards the center. You're actually moving their fulcrum back to the center. Not forever, but just for those fleeting moments and bam, because of that the scale plops down because suddenly those positives have massive power and the negatives are not quite as powerful. That's why when you lower a prospect's action threshold, if you've gotten to a really high level certainty, that's why it is so profound, so effective. Now again, lowering their action threshold using that strategy, well that was one of four, but there's also now this next way, which is using pain. See what happens is this. Remember I told you the story about my dad, how he's got the highest action threshold of the world, right? But one day we were driving the car down to Florida from New York on a family vacation and all of a sudden we were like about 200 miles from home, the sun is setting and the water heater breaks, car is steaming, my father's flipping out, sun is setting, we're really far from home, his family's in danger, despite how much he loved his car, in that moment the pain, the worry of not being close to home, he was done. His action threshold dropped straight down to the floor and just like that he was transformed, literally transformed, this is the right word, into one of the easiest buyers in the world. That's the power of using pain to lower action threshold even more. So again when we add on pain, we're not really making changes to how certain someone is, we change how much their collective certainty matters to them in terms of how much force it exerts on their buying decision. That's why it works so well. So this is why now, think it through, it's why we introduce pain not in one but in two spots. First, we increase pain, we ratchet up pain during the intelligence gathering phase, you want to identify where your prospects pain lies and if necessary you want to amplify that pain somewhat to ensure that they're listening to your presentation from the appropriate perspective, meaning they're not just in denial and tuned out because they feel alright. So you want to get some pain there, we fix them, oh wow, they want to hang on your every word. And then second and most importantly, you only to introduce that pain once more right now at the beginning of your third loop. So here we are, we're on our third loop, what do we do? Well always, always, always, always we start our loop by answering whatever objection they hit you with. You hit with an objection, that's how a loop starts. So you start by answering that objection which everyone may hop to using more than dozens of proven rebuttals that I've given you in the resource center, you have tons of those right, albeit with a task of understanding that no matter how awesome your rebuttal might sound, the only thing the rebuttal does is it earns you the right to speak more. It's what you say after the rebuttal that's going to persuade your prospect to buy. So again, after you answer that objection, you're now going to go about rationing up their pain threshold using a language pattern that sounds something like this. You'll say, now Bill, let me ask you, as you said before, that you're worried about your retirement, I'm just making it up because it could change based on what you're selling. You say you're worried about your retirement in terms of your social security and your pension, not digging enough to cover you and your family and then what you're doing is you're digging into the pain he revealed to you during prospecting. So you take that pain and now you say, so tell me Bill, you know, where do you see that leaving you in three years now if you don't do anything or six months from now? If you don't take action to fix it. For example, by using an empathetic, sympathetic tone, you can stay in rapport and really dig into that and say, you know, is it going to deteriorate over the 12 months? Where is that going to leave you? Where do you see yourself in a year from now or worse, five years from now? How will your family survive? And again, you want to be very careful. You're not looking to disempower or hurt someone. You want to wake up that denial because people go into denial as a self-protective mechanism. They feel pain for too long and just can't live your life. So what do you do? You bury, you submerge the pain and you say, you know what? Yeah, my life might stink. I might not have the things I really need, but it's better than driving myself crazy. You convince yourself you're okay. We all do this as human beings. We down regularly bury our pain and say, we're okay. Well, let me say this. There's nothing that you could do that's more ethical than to elegantly and kindly wake someone up from this slumber of denial and let them realize, you know what? Wow, my life isn't the way it should be. There are things that are missing from life. Let me tell you, I watched my dad and my mom, brilliant, hardworking people, educated people go through life lacking so many different things. Why? This ultra high action, they both shared action, both of them had that, right? It stopped them from filling needs. They went through life with unfulfilled needs and rationalized it out using denial. Very powerful stuff. And by the way, here's what happens. Nine times out of 10, if you do this, if you actually ask your prospect this question using the right tonality, they'll probably say, well, it's probably going to be a lot worse. And they'll start picking at their own scabs. Human beings love little po-po-po-po. They'll torture them. They'll torture them. So, oh, you're right. And they start going and going. And you're like, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. Use your active listening to get an even tighter reply. I understand you. Mm-hmm, mm-hmm. Yup. And ask them follow-up questions. And by doing that, you wake up that pain threshold, right? It goes flying up. What happens to the fulcrum? It slides back to the center. And then you button up, starting a bit more head-on, some certainty. And the scale drops. And you close the deal. That's powerful stuff. And with that, I want to stop. Take a quick quiz here. Want you to go through your exercises to lock down these patterns because we're going to be using them to ratchet up pain in our prospects going forward. Very important thing. But first, before I get specific now, the next lesson is going to be on actually answering individual objections and how do you separate those out from what I call buy signals. In other words, you're at that point right now in the sale where you're going to start getting a lot of buy signals, very different than objections. So, as soon as you've done what you're quizzing your exercises, meet me in the next lesson. I'm going to take a very important lesson. And then after that, I'll get specific on how you actually execute this third loop, which gives you number five, that number of your combination, locks it down tight, and then you're set up for absolute greatness.
